content.text: Modern workflows rarely live in a single interface. Claude's growing ecosystem of third-party integrations brings its language capabilities directly into the tools where work already happens — editors, communication platforms, knowledge bases, and productivity suites — so that the AI capability is available without switching context.
What Claude in Apps Means

Claude in Apps refers to official and community-built integrations that embed Claude's capabilities into external software products. Rather than visiting Claude.ai directly, users interact with Claude from within a platform they already use — Slack for team communication, VS Code for software development, Notion for documentation, or Zapier for workflow automation.

These integrations fall into several categories. Some are first-party extensions published by Anthropic or the app vendor. Others are community-built plugins or middleware that connect Claude via Anthropic's public API. A growing number are Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, which let Claude directly read from and write to external systems in a standardized way.

The core mechanism is always the same: the host application makes calls to Anthropic's API with the user's prompt as input, receives a completion, and surfaces the result inside its own UI. What varies is how much of the host's data context is bundled with that prompt, and how the response is rendered or acted upon.
Why It Matters

Context switching is one of the most significant productivity drains in knowledge work. When AI assistance requires opening a separate tab, copying content, pasting a prompt, reading the result, and copying it back, much of the time saving is lost to overhead. Embedding Claude directly in existing tools eliminates that friction.

Beyond convenience, in-app integration enables tighter workflows. A VS Code extension can read your open file before you write any prompt. A Notion integration can access the page you are editing as context. A Slack bot can reference thread history when composing a reply. These integrations can provide richer context than a standalone chat session because they have access to data that is right there in the application.

For teams, in-app Claude integrations also reduce the learning curve. Not every team member will set up API access or develop prompt-engineering discipline; but if Claude is available as a button or slash command inside Slack or Notion, the barrier to adoption is minimal.
Major Integrations Covered in This Guide
Slack
— conversational AI via a Slack bot, useful for drafting messages, summarising threads, and answering questions
VS Code
— code generation, explanation, and refactoring from within the editor
Notion
— writing assistance, content generation, and database summarisation inside documents
Cursor
— AI-native editor that puts Claude at the center of the coding experience
Zapier / Make
— no-code automation that connects Claude to thousands of other apps
GitHub Copilot Chat (with Claude backend)
— code-aware chat within GitHub and VS Code
JetBrains IDEs
— AI assistance inside IntelliJ, PyCharm, WebStorm, and other JetBrains products
Raycast
— macOS launcher with Claude commands reachable from any app
Architecture of a Typical Integration

attrs.alt: Flow from user request through app layer to Claude API and back

content.text: Every integration follows a request-response cycle. The host application captures the user's action — typing a slash command, clicking a button, or selecting a code block — and constructs an API request. That request includes the user's prompt plus any context the integration injects automatically, such as file contents, page text, or conversation history.

The request goes to Anthropic's API, which returns a streaming or complete response. The host application then renders that response in its own UI — inserting it as a Notion block, posting it as a Slack message, or streaming it as code into the editor.

More advanced integrations use Anthropic's tool use feature to let Claude take actions: calling the host app's own API to create a document, close a ticket, or schedule a calendar event. When combined with MCP servers, Claude can interact with a wide range of external data sources with structured I/O.
Claude in Slack
Setting Up the Slack Integration

Anthropic offers an official Claude for Work integration available in the Slack App Directory. Once installed by a workspace administrator, any member can invoke Claude using the @Claude mention or by opening the Claude direct-message channel.
Open the Slack App Directory and search for Claude.
Click Add to Slack and follow the OAuth authorization flow.
After installation, the Claude app appears in the left sidebar under Apps.
Send it a direct message or mention @Claude in any channel where the bot has been invited.
Using Claude in Slack

Claude in Slack supports both direct conversation and in-channel assistance. Common use cases include summarizing long threads, drafting replies, answering questions about company topics if configured with context, and generating structured content like meeting agendas or status reports.

# Summarize a thread
@Claude Summarize the main decisions made in this thread.

# Draft a reply
@Claude Draft a polite follow-up to the message above asking for a status update by Friday.

# Answer a question in a DM
What is the difference between REST and GraphQL? Give me a concise answer.

# Generate content
Create a short agenda for our Monday standup with sections for blockers, progress, and next steps.

Claude in Slack does not have access to your workspace's documents or files by default, only to messages in threads where it is mentioned and the content you paste directly. For document-level context, the integration would need to be combined with a Slack workflow that fetches and attaches content from another source.
Claude in VS Code
Setting Up VS Code Extensions

Several extensions bring Claude to VS Code. The most direct option is the official Claude Dev extension (also known as Cline), which supports the full Anthropic API and can read and write files, run terminal commands, and interact with the VS Code workspace. Cursor is an alternative: a VS Code fork that ships with deep Claude integration built in.
Open VS Code and navigate to the Extensions panel (Ctrl+Shift+X / Cmd+Shift+X).
Search for Cline or Claude Dev.
Install the extension and open its settings panel.
Enter your Anthropic API key in the provider settings.
Select Claude 3.5 Sonnet or another model as the default.
Common Workflows in VS Code

With a Claude extension active in VS Code you can select a function, press the extension's keybinding, and ask Claude to explain, refactor, add tests, or fix a bug. The extension typically injects the selected code and the surrounding file into the prompt automatically.

# Typical extension interaction (typed in the extension's chat panel)

User: The function below has an off-by-one error. Fix it and explain what was wrong.
[selected code is automatically attached]

Claude: The issue is on line 12 where the loop condition uses `<= arr.length`...

# Generating new code
User: Add a TypeScript interface for the User object and update the function signature.

# Asking about project-wide patterns
User: What pattern does this project use for error handling? Look at the catch blocks in this file.
Cursor — the AI-Native Editor

Cursor is a VS Code fork built around AI assistance. It connects to Claude (and other models) and provides composer mode for multi-file edits, inline edit (Ctrl+K) for quick changes, and a chat panel that has read access to the entire codebase index. Many developers use Cursor as their primary editor because the integration is tighter than any plugin: Cursor indexes the repository and can answer questions about code it has never opened in an active tab.
Claude in Notion
Enabling Notion AI with Claude

Notion AI uses Claude as its underlying language model for its premium AI assistant tier. When Notion AI is enabled on a workspace, all members with access can use it to generate text, summarise pages, extract action items from meeting notes, translate content, and more — all without leaving the document.
Open Notion workspace settings.
Navigate to the Notion AI section and enable it (requires a paid plan with AI add-on).
In any page, press Space at the beginning of a block to open the AI menu, or use the /AI slash command.
Useful Notion AI Workflows
Summarise a long meeting-notes page into three bullet points
Translate a paragraph from English to French without leaving the page
Generate a project brief from a bullet list of requirements
Extract action items from a messy meeting transcript and format them as checkboxes
Improve the writing of a draft paragraph for clarity and concision
Create a table comparing multiple options from free-form notes

Notion AI works on the content of the current page. It does not automatically access linked databases or other pages unless you explicitly paste their content into context. For complex knowledge-base tasks, manually providing context in the prompt yields better results.
Claude via Zapier and Make
No-Code Automation

Zapier and Make (formerly Integromat) are workflow-automation platforms that connect hundreds of apps through a visual builder. Both have native Claude or Anthropic integrations, allowing anyone to build automations that pass data to Claude and use the output in other tools — without writing any code.
Example Zapier Automation: Email Summarizer
Create a new Zap with Gmail as the trigger (new email matching a filter).
Add an Anthropic action step: choose the 'Create Message' action.
In the prompt field, use Zapier's variable syntax to include the email body: 'Summarize the following email in two sentences: {{body}}'.
Add a Google Sheets action that appends a row with the sender, subject, and Claude's summary.
Enable the Zap and test it with a real email.

# Zapier Anthropic action configuration
Model:       claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022
Max tokens:  300
Prompt:      Summarize the following customer support email in 2 sentences.
             Identify the customer's main issue and emotional tone.

             Email: {{1.body_plain}}

# The output {{2.content}} can then be used in subsequent steps:
# - Append to a Google Sheet log
# - Post to a #support-summary Slack channel
# - Create a Notion database entry
Make (Integromat) Scenarios

Make supports more complex branching logic than Zapier. A scenario could route Claude's output through an if/else branch: if the output contains 'urgent', create a high-priority Jira ticket; otherwise append to a backlog document. This kind of conditional AI-driven routing is powerful for triage workflows.
Claude in JetBrains IDEs

The official Claude AI plugin for JetBrains IDEs (IntelliJ IDEA, PyCharm, WebStorm, GoLand, etc.) is available through the JetBrains Marketplace. After installation and API key configuration, Claude appears as a side panel that can read the currently open file, selected code, and project structure.
Open the JetBrains Marketplace (Settings > Plugins > Marketplace).
Search for Claude AI or Anthropic.
Install the plugin and restart the IDE.
Open the plugin panel and paste your Anthropic API key in the settings.
Select a code block and use the panel to ask questions or request changes.

JetBrains' deep project indexing means Claude can often receive more structured context about the project — including module structure and type information — than a standalone chat would get from a manually pasted snippet.
Claude in Raycast

Raycast is a macOS launcher that replaces Spotlight. The Raycast AI extension, configurable to use Claude as the backend, makes Claude accessible from any application via a global keyboard shortcut. Users can highlight text in any app, invoke Raycast, and run AI commands like 'Improve writing', 'Translate to Spanish', or 'Fix grammar' without switching context at all.

The {selection} placeholder injects whatever text is selected in the current application. This turns Claude into a system-wide AI tool that works equally well in email clients, documents, browsers, and terminals.
Model Context Protocol (MCP) Integrations

MCP is Anthropic's open protocol for connecting AI models to external data sources and tools in a structured way. An MCP server exposes resources (data Claude can read) and tools (actions Claude can take), and a compatible client — such as Claude.ai's desktop app or a developer's custom setup — can call these servers during a conversation.

MCP servers exist for file systems, databases, GitHub, Slack, Google Drive, web browsers, and many other targets. When an MCP server is connected, Claude can read files from your filesystem, query a database, fetch a GitHub issue, or search Slack messages as part of answering a question — all within a single conversation turn.

With the filesystem MCP server configured and the Claude desktop app connected to it, a user can ask 'What does the auth module do?' and Claude will read the relevant files from the local path before answering — without any manual copy-paste.
When to Use In-App Integrations vs. Claude.ai
The choice between using Claude via an in-app integration and using Claude.ai directly depends on what data Claude needs and what action should be taken with the result.
Scenario
Best Approach
Coding in an editor
VS Code / Cursor extension — file context is automatic
Team communication in Slack
Slack bot — no context switching needed
Writing or research in Notion
Notion AI — page content is available in-context
Multi-step automation
Zapier / Make — connects Claude to other apps
Quick one-off tasks
Claude.ai — faster setup, more model options
Cross-app file access
MCP servers in Claude desktop app
Highly custom prompting
Direct API or Claude.ai — full control over system prompt
Common Mistakes and Pitfalls
Assuming the Integration Sends Full Context

Many integrations inject only the immediately visible content, not the full document, codebase, or conversation history. Before blaming Claude for a shallow answer, verify what context the integration is actually sending. Most have a debug or log mode that shows the raw API payload.
Ignoring Rate Limits and Costs

In-app integrations often make API calls automatically and frequently. A Zapier automation that summarizes every incoming email can accumulate significant token usage quickly, especially if emails are long. Always set Anthropic API usage limits and monitor spending in the Anthropic Console.
Sharing Sensitive Data Without Review

Integrations that automatically send file contents or database records to Claude should be reviewed for data sensitivity. Check Anthropic's data privacy policy and any enterprise agreements before routing confidential business data through a Claude integration, especially in shared automation workflows.
Best Practices for Using Claude in Apps
Start with the simplest integration
that meets the need. A Raycast command or Zapier automation is often faster to set up than a custom integration.
Prefer integrations that inject context automatically
rather than requiring manual copy-paste; the value of in-app access is reduced context switching.
Test what context the integration sends
by checking its settings, reading its docs, or inspecting the API call. Knowing what Claude sees helps you phrase prompts more effectively.
Monitor API usage
from the Anthropic Console to prevent unexpected bills, especially for automations that run on triggers.
Use system prompts when available
to give Claude a consistent persona and operating constraints within the integration's context.
Verify outputs before acting on them
especially in automated workflows where Claude's output drives a downstream action like creating a ticket or sending an email.
Performance and Scalability Considerations

In-app integrations inherit the latency and throughput constraints of Anthropic's API. Response times for Claude 3.5 Sonnet typically range from 1–10 seconds for short prompts, longer for extended responses. For synchronous integrations — where the user waits in the UI — this is generally acceptable. For high-volume automations, batch processing or asynchronous patterns reduce the perceived wait.

If an integration needs to run Claude for hundreds of documents simultaneously, consider using Anthropic's batch API rather than individual requests. This allows large volumes of requests to be queued and processed at reduced cost and priority, which is appropriate for non-real-time workflows like nightly report generation.
Security and Reliability

In-app Claude integrations introduce security considerations that standalone chat does not. The API key must be stored securely — never hardcoded in source code or committed to repositories. For team workflows, use environment variables, secrets managers, or the integration platform's own secrets storage.

Reliability is also a concern for production automations. Claude API availability is high but not guaranteed to be 100%. Automations that depend on Claude should handle API errors gracefully — logging failures, sending alerts, and queuing retries with exponential backoff — rather than silently dropping work or crashing the entire pipeline.
Comparison with Alternatives

Cross-system tasks
Key Takeaways
Claude in Apps embeds Claude's capabilities inside existing tools like Slack, VS Code, Notion, and automation platforms, reducing context-switching friction.
Every integration works by sending user content and injected context to the Anthropic API and rendering Claude's response inside the host application's UI.
The Slack integration handles team communication; VS Code and Cursor extensions handle coding; Notion AI handles document work; Zapier and Make handle no-code automations.
Model Context Protocol (MCP) is Anthropic's open standard for giving Claude structured access to external data and tools, enabling richer in-app experiences.
In-app integrations only send what they can see — always verify what context an integration injects before assuming Claude has access to your full workspace.
Monitor API usage and costs when building or using automated integrations that call Claude on triggers.
Store API keys securely and design automations to handle API errors gracefully with retries and fallbacks.
